problemTitle: Reverse Odd Levels of Binary Tree

sources.code:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(n)

# Definition for a binary tree node.
class TreeNode(object):
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        pass


# bfs
class Solution(object):
    def reverseOddLevels(self, root):
        """
        :type root: Optional[TreeNode]
        :rtype: Optional[TreeNode]
        """
        q = [root]
        parity = 0
        while q:
            if parity:
                left, right = 0, len(q)-1
                while left < right:
                    q[left].val, q[right].val = q[right].val, q[left].val
                    left += 1
                    right -= 1
            if not q[0].left:
                break
            new_q = []
            for node in q:
                new_q.append(node.left)
                new_q.append(node.right)
            q = new_q       
            parity ^= 1
        return root

# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(n)

# Definition for a binary tree node.
class TreeNode(object):
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        pass


# bfs
class Solution(object):
    def reverseOddLevels(self, root):
        """
        :type root: Optional[TreeNode]
        :rtype: Optional[TreeNode]
        """
        q = [root]
        parity = 0
        while q:
            if parity:
                left, right = 0, len(q)-1
                while left < right:
                    q[left].val, q[right].val = q[right].val, q[left].val
                    left += 1
                    right -= 1
            if not q[0].left:
                break
            new_q = []
            for node in q:
                new_q.append(node.left)
                new_q.append(node.right)
            q = new_q       
            parity ^= 1
        return root
sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(n)

# Definition for a binary tree node.
class TreeNode(object):
    # [Reader note] Entry point
    # Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Do the traversal first, understand the level as a batch, then apply the parity rule to that batch only.
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        pass


# bfs
class Solution(object):
    def reverseOddLevels(self, root):
        """
        :type root: Optional[TreeNode]
        :rtype: Optional[TreeNode]
        """
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Level grouping, per-level buffering, and modifying tree values without losing track of level structure.
        q = [root]
        parity = 0
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while q:
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The trap is not algorithmic difficulty; it is mixing up which nodes belong to one logical level before you reverse anything.
            if parity:
                left, right = 0, len(q)-1
                while left < right:
                    q[left].val, q[right].val = q[right].val, q[left].val
                    left += 1
                    right -= 1
            if not q[0].left:
                break
            new_q = []
            for node in q:
                new_q.append(node.left)
                new_q.append(node.right)
            q = new_q       
            parity ^= 1
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return root

sources.relativePath: solution/2400-2499/2415.Reverse Odd Levels of Binary Tree/Solution.py
sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Do the traversal first, understand the level as a batch, then apply the parity rule to that batch only.
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Level grouping, per-level buffering, and modifying tree values without losing track of level structure.
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right
class Solution:
    def reverseOddLevels(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> Optional[TreeNode]:
        q = deque([root])
        i = 0
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while q:
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The trap is not algorithmic difficulty; it is mixing up which nodes belong to one logical level before you reverse anything.
            if i & 1:
                l, r = 0, len(q) - 1
                while l < r:
                    q[l].val, q[r].val = q[r].val, q[l].val
                    l, r = l + 1, r - 1
            for _ in range(len(q)):
                node = q.popleft()
                if node.left:
                    q.append(node.left)
                    q.append(node.right)
            i += 1
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return root
